text: We describe a system that participated in the SemEval-2013 task on Sentiment Analysis in Twitter. Our system is designed for two tasks: (a) classifying the sentiment of short informal texts like tweets and SMS (the message-level task) and (b) classifying the sentiment of individual words or phrases within a given message (the term-level task). Our system is based on a supervised learning statistical approach to text classification, and it uses a variety of surface-form, semantic and sentiment features for training a classifier. Most of the sentiment features are based on two tweet-specific sentiment lexicons that were constructed automatically from a subset of the training set that includes tweets with sentiment-word hashtags, and from all tweets with emoticons as they are strong indicators of sentiment. A third lexicon is constructed for the negated words in order to properly classify the sentiment of the words in the negated contexts. We have tested our system finish as the first in the SemEval-2013 task 2 (Message-Level Sentiment Analysis and Task 3 (Term-Level Sentiment Analysis) with an F-score of 69.02 on the message-level task and 88.93 on the term-level task, but after some post-experiment adjustments we were able to reach an F-score of 70.45 in the message-level task and 89.50 in the term-level task. We also tested the system on the SemEval-2013 SMS test set and a corpus of movie review excerpts, for which it achieved the highest scores in the literature, indicating the strength of our approach. We also demonstrated with ablation experiments the contribution of the automatically generated lexicons that we used in our system, the improvements that we were able to achieve due to the use of these lexicons are up to 6.5 absolute percentage points.
